subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following conditions regarding a chemical process ensures its spontaneity at all temperature?
question: Which of the following conditions regarding a chemical process ensures its spontaneity at all temperature?
solution:
For a chemical process to be spontaneous, its Gibbs free energy change must be negative:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

For spontaneity at all temperatures, $\Delta G$ should remain negative for every value of $T$.

Now check the signs.

If

$$ \Delta H < 0 $$

the enthalpy term favours spontaneity.

If

$$ \Delta S > 0 $$

then $-T\Delta S$ is also negative at all temperatures, because $T$ is always positive in Kelvin.

So both terms in

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

are negative:

$$ \Delta G = \text{negative} - \text{positive} $$

Hence, $\Delta G$ is always negative at all temperatures.

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B: } \Delta H < 0,\ \Delta S > 0} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is incorrect regarding Gibbs free energy
question: Which of the following is incorrect regarding Gibbs free energy
solution:
Gibbs free energy is given by

$$ G = H - TS $$

Here, $H$ is enthalpy, $T$ is temperature, and $S$ is entropy.

Now, Gibbs free energy is:

  • a state function, because its value depends only on the state of the system, not on the path followed.

  • an extensive property, because it depends on the amount of substance present.

  • a macroscopic property, because it describes the bulk behaviour of the system.

But Gibbs free energy itself is not an intensive property.

Only molar Gibbs free energy, $\frac{G}{n}$, is intensive.

So the incorrect statement is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: it is intensive property}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following reaction is at equilibrium at 298 K

2NO (g, 0.00001 bar) + Cl2 (g, 0.01 bar) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NOCl (g, 0.01 bar).

$ \Delta G^{\circ} $ for the reaction is
question:
The following reaction is at equilibrium at 298 K

2NO (g, 0.00001 bar) + Cl2 (g, 0.01 bar) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NOCl (g, 0.01 bar).

$ \Delta G^{\circ} $ for the reaction is
solution:
For the reaction,

$$ 2NO(g)+Cl_2(g)\rightleftharpoons 2NOCl(g) $$

At equilibrium,

$$ \Delta G = 0 $$

Using the relation,

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q $$

At equilibrium, $Q=K_p$, so

$$ 0=\Delta G^\circ + RT\ln K_p $$

Hence,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K_p $$

Now calculate $K_p$:

$$ K_p=\frac{(p_{NOCl})^2}{(p_{NO})^2(p_{Cl_2})} $$

Given:

$$ p_{NOCl}=0.01 \text{ bar}=10^{-2}\text{ bar} $$

$$ p_{NO}=0.00001 \text{ bar}=10^{-5}\text{ bar} $$

$$ p_{Cl_2}=0.01 \text{ bar}=10^{-2}\text{ bar} $$

So,

$$ K_p=\frac{(10^{-2})^2}{(10^{-5})^2(10^{-2})} $$

$$ K_p=\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-10}\times 10^{-2}} $$

$$ K_p=\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-12}} $$

$$ K_p=10^8 $$

Now,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K_p $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -(8.314)(298)\ln(10^8) $$

Since,

$$ \ln(10^8)=8\ln 10=8\times 2.303 $$

$$ \ln(10^8)=18.424 $$

So,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -(8.314)(298)(18.424) $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ \approx -45650 \text{ J mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ \approx -45.65 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{-45.65\text{ kJ}} $$

Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q:
If $\Delta G^\circ = -177$ K cal for

(1) $2\ \mathrm{Fe}(s) + \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s)$

and $\Delta G^\circ = -19$ K cal for

(2) $4\ \mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s) + \mathrm{Fe}(s) \rightarrow 3\ \mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s)$

What is the Gibbs free energy of formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s)$?
question:
If $\Delta G^\circ = -177$ K cal for

(1) $2\ \mathrm{Fe}(s) + \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s)$

and $\Delta G^\circ = -19$ K cal for

(2) $4\ \mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s) + \mathrm{Fe}(s) \rightarrow 3\ \mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s)$

What is the Gibbs free energy of formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s)$?
solution:
The Gibbs free energy of formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s)$ means the value of $\Delta G^\circ$ for the reaction:

$$ 3\mathrm{Fe}(s)+2\mathrm{O}_2(g)\rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s) $$

Given:

$$ 2\mathrm{Fe}(s)+\frac{3}{2}\mathrm{O}_2(g)\rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s) $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-177\ \text{kcal} $$

Multiply this reaction by $4$:

$$ 8\mathrm{Fe}(s)+6\mathrm{O}_2(g)\rightarrow 4\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s) $$

So,

$$ \Delta G^\circ=4(-177)=-708\ \text{kcal} $$

Now add the second reaction:

$$ 4\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3(s)+\mathrm{Fe}(s)\rightarrow 3\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4(s) $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-19\ \text{kcal} $$

Adding both reactions:

$$ 8\mathrm{Fe}+6\mathrm{O}_2+4\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{Fe} \rightarrow 4\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3+3\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4 $$

Cancel $4\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ from both sides:

$$ 9\mathrm{Fe}+6\mathrm{O}_2\rightarrow 3\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4 $$

Total Gibbs free energy:

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-708-19=-727\ \text{kcal} $$

This is for formation of $3$ moles of $\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4$.

For $1$ mole:

$$ \Delta G_f^\circ=\frac{-727}{3} $$

$$ \Delta G_f^\circ=-242.3\ \text{kcal mol}^{-1} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{-242.3\ \text{kcal/mol}} $$

Option B.

subject: Chemistry
q: The enthalpy and entropy change for a chemical reaction are $-2500$ cal and $+7.4$ cal/K, respectively. The nature of reaction at 298 K is
question: The enthalpy and entropy change for a chemical reaction are $-2500$ cal and $+7.4$ cal/K, respectively. The nature of reaction at 298 K is
solution:
For deciding whether a reaction is spontaneous at a given temperature, we use Gibbs free energy change:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

Given,

$$ \Delta H = -2500 \text{ cal} $$

$$ \Delta S = +7.4 \text{ cal K}^{-1} $$

$$ T = 298 \text{ K} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ \Delta G = -2500 - 298(7.4) $$

$$ \Delta G = -2500 - 2205.2 $$

$$ \Delta G = -4705.2 \text{ cal} $$

Since $\Delta G$ is negative, the reaction is spontaneous at $298 \text{ K}$.

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A: spontaneous}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reaction A(g) ⇌ B(g) at equilibrium. The partial pressure of B is found to be one fourth of the partial pressure of A. The value of $\Delta G^\circ$ of the reaction A → B is
question: For a reaction A(g) ⇌ B(g) at equilibrium. The partial pressure of B is found to be one fourth of the partial pressure of A. The value of $\Delta G^\circ$ of the reaction A → B is
solution:
For the reaction

$$ A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g) $$

the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressure is

$$ K_p=\frac{p_B}{p_A} $$

It is given that the partial pressure of $B$ is one fourth of the partial pressure of $A$:

$$ p_B=\frac{1}{4}p_A $$

So,

$$ K_p=\frac{p_B}{p_A}=\frac{1}{4} $$

For a reaction, the relation between standard Gibbs energy change and equilibrium constant is

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-RT\ln K $$

So,

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-RT\ln\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) $$

Since

$$ \ln\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=-\ln 4 $$

we get

$$ \Delta G^\circ=RT\ln 4 $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A: } RT\ln 4} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: When reaction in standard state is at equilibrium, then :
question: When reaction in standard state is at equilibrium, then :
solution:
When a reaction is in equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy change is zero:

$$ \Delta G = 0 $$

For a reaction in the standard state, all reactants and products are in their standard states, so the reaction quotient is

$$ Q = 1 $$

Using the relation

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q $$

Since $Q = 1$,

$$ \ln 1 = 0 $$

So,

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ $$

At equilibrium,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = 0 $$

Now using the NCERT relation:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K $$

Since $\Delta G^\circ = 0$,

$$ 0 = -RT \ln K $$

So,

$$ \ln K = 0 $$

Hence,

$$ K = 1 $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: equilibrium constant } K = 1} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A reaction has $\Delta H = -33\ \text{kJ}$ and $\Delta S = -58\ \text{J/K}$. This reaction would be:
question: A reaction has $\Delta H = -33\ \text{kJ}$ and $\Delta S = -58\ \text{J/K}$. This reaction would be:
solution:
For spontaneity, we use Gibbs energy change:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

A reaction is spontaneous when:

$$ \Delta G < 0 $$

Here,

$$ \Delta H = -33\ \text{kJ} $$

and

$$ \Delta S = -58\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Since $\Delta S$ is in joules, convert $\Delta H$ into joules:

$$ \Delta H = -33000\ \text{J} $$

Now substitute the signs in the formula:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

Since $\Delta H$ is negative and $\Delta S$ is negative,

$$ \Delta G = (-) - T(-) $$

So,

$$ \Delta G = (-) + (+) $$

This means the reaction has two opposing effects:

  • $\Delta H < 0$ favours spontaneity.

  • $\Delta S < 0$ does not favour spontaneity, especially at high temperature.

At low temperature, the $T\Delta S$ term is small, so the negative $\Delta H$ dominates. Hence $\Delta G$ is negative and the reaction is spontaneous.

At high temperature, the positive contribution from $-T\Delta S$ becomes large, so $\Delta G$ becomes positive. Then the reaction becomes non-spontaneous.

The temperature at which the change occurs is:

$$ \Delta G = 0 $$

$$ T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} $$

$$ T = \frac{-33000}{-58} $$

$$ T \approx 569\ \text{K} $$

So the reaction is spontaneous below about $569\ \text{K}$.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: spontaneous below a certain temperature}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
S$_2$(s) + 2O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2SO$_2$(g); $\Delta G = -544$ kJ

2Zn(s) + S$_2$(s) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnS(s); $\Delta G = -293$ kJ

2Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO(s); $\Delta G = -480$ kJ

For the reaction given below, the value of $\Delta G$

2ZnS(s) + 3O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO(s) + 2SO$_2$(g)

is
question:
S$_2$(s) + 2O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2SO$_2$(g); $\Delta G = -544$ kJ

2Zn(s) + S$_2$(s) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnS(s); $\Delta G = -293$ kJ

2Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO(s); $\Delta G = -480$ kJ

For the reaction given below, the value of $\Delta G$

2ZnS(s) + 3O$_2$(g) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO(s) + 2SO$_2$(g)

is
solution:
Since $\Delta G$ is a state function, we can add the given reactions in such a way that we get the required reaction.

Required reaction is:

$$ 2ZnS(s) + 3O_2(g) \rightarrow 2ZnO(s) + 2SO_2(g) $$

We need $2ZnS$ on the reactant side, so reverse the second reaction:

$$ 2ZnS(s) \rightarrow 2Zn(s) + S_2(s) $$

So its $\Delta G$ becomes:

$$ \Delta G = +293 \text{ kJ} $$

Now take the third reaction as it is:

$$ 2Zn(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow 2ZnO(s) $$

$$ \Delta G = -480 \text{ kJ} $$

Also take the first reaction as it is:

$$ S_2(s) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow 2SO_2(g) $$

$$ \Delta G = -544 \text{ kJ} $$

Now add these three reactions:

$$ 2ZnS(s) \rightarrow 2Zn(s) + S_2(s) $$

$$ 2Zn(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow 2ZnO(s) $$

$$ S_2(s) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow 2SO_2(g) $$

On cancelling $2Zn(s)$ and $S_2(s)$, we get:

$$ 2ZnS(s) + 3O_2(g) \rightarrow 2ZnO(s) + 2SO_2(g) $$

Now add the $\Delta G$ values:

$$ \Delta G = 293 - 480 - 544 $$

$$ \Delta G = -731 \text{ kJ} $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{-731 \text{ kJ}} $$

Option B.

subject: Chemistry
q: For a particular reaction $\Delta H^\circ = -76.6 \text{ kJ}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = 226 \text{ J K}^{-1}$. This reaction is:
question: For a particular reaction $\Delta H^\circ = -76.6 \text{ kJ}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = 226 \text{ J K}^{-1}$. This reaction is:
solution:
For spontaneity, we use Gibbs energy:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ $$

A reaction is spontaneous when:

$$ \Delta G^\circ < 0 $$

Here,

$$ \Delta H^\circ = -76.6\ \text{kJ} $$

and

$$ \Delta S^\circ = 226\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

First convert entropy into kJ:

$$ 226\ \text{J K}^{-1} = 0.226\ \text{kJ K}^{-1} $$

So,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -76.6 - T(0.226) $$

Since temperature $T$ is always positive on the Kelvin scale, the term $T(0.226)$ is positive.

So,

$$ - T(0.226) $$

will always be negative.

That means:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = \text{negative} - \text{positive} $$

So $\Delta G^\circ$ will always be negative at all temperatures.

Hence, the reaction is spontaneous at all temperatures.

Correct option: Option A

$$ \boxed{\text{Spontaneous at all temperatures}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The standard entropy change (in cal/K-mol) for the reaction: $\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y}$, if the value of $\Delta H^{\circ}=7.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $K_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{e}^{-10}$ at 300 K .
question: The standard entropy change (in cal/K-mol) for the reaction: $\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y}$, if the value of $\Delta H^{\circ}=7.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $K_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{e}^{-10}$ at 300 K .
solution:
Using the relation between free energy and the equilibrium constant:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_C$$

Here $R = 2$ cal/K-mol (since we want the answer in calories), $T = 300$ K, and $\ln K_C = -10$.

$$\Delta G^\circ = -(2)(300)(-10) = 6000 \text{ cal/mol} = 6 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

Now applying $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$:

$$6 = 7.5 - 300 \cdot \Delta S^\circ \times 10^{-3}$$

Working in calories directly:

$$6000 = 7500 - 300 \, \Delta S^\circ$$

$$300 \, \Delta S^\circ = 1500$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = 5 \text{ cal/K-mol}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: For 13 g of ethanol (density $=0.78 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ ), the rise in Gibbs free energy (in kJ) when its pressure is changed isothermally from 1 bar to 3001 bar, is
question: For 13 g of ethanol (density $=0.78 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ ), the rise in Gibbs free energy (in kJ) when its pressure is changed isothermally from 1 bar to 3001 bar, is
solution:
For a liquid, the volume changes very little with pressure, so we can take volume as constant.

At constant temperature,

$$ dG = V\,dP $$

So,

$$ \Delta G = V(P_2-P_1) $$

Given mass of ethanol:

$$ m = 13 \text{ g} $$

Density:

$$ \rho = 0.78 \text{ g cm}^{-3} $$

Volume of ethanol:

$$ V=\frac{m}{\rho} $$

$$ V=\frac{13}{0.78}=16.67 \text{ cm}^3 $$

Convert this into $\text{m}^3$:

$$ 16.67 \text{ cm}^3 = 16.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 $$

Pressure change:

$$ \Delta P = 3001 - 1 = 3000 \text{ bar} $$

Since,

$$ 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} $$

$$ \Delta P = 3000 \times 10^5 = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} $$

Now,

$$ \Delta G = V\Delta P $$

$$ \Delta G = 16.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^8 $$

$$ \Delta G = 5.0 \times 10^3 \text{ J} $$

$$ \Delta G = 5 \text{ kJ} $$

So, the increase in Gibbs free energy is

$$ \boxed{5 \text{ kJ}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the free energy change $(\Delta G)$ when 1.0 mole of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure is converted into steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure ?
question: What is the free energy change $(\Delta G)$ when 1.0 mole of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure is converted into steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure ?
solution:
At $100^\circ \mathrm{C}$ and $1$ atm pressure, water and steam are in equilibrium.

For a phase change at constant temperature and pressure,

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

During vaporisation at the boiling point,

$$ \Delta S = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T} $$

So,

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H_{\text{vap}} - T \left(\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T}\right) $$

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H_{\text{vap}} - \Delta H_{\text{vap}} $$

$$ \Delta G = 0 $$

Hence, when $1.0$ mole of water at $100^\circ \mathrm{C}$ and $1$ atm is converted into steam at the same temperature and pressure,

$$ \boxed{\Delta G = 0} $$

This is because at the normal boiling point, liquid water and steam coexist in equilibrium, so the free energy change for the conversion is zero.

subject: Chemistry
q: Calculate the energy (in kJ ) that can be used to support muscular and nervous activity when 1.0 mole of glucose molecules undergoes combustion under standard conditions at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (body temperature). For the reaction, the standard entropy change and enthalpy change are $+200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}-\mathrm{mol}$ and $-2808 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.
question: Calculate the energy (in kJ ) that can be used to support muscular and nervous activity when 1.0 mole of glucose molecules undergoes combustion under standard conditions at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (body temperature). For the reaction, the standard entropy change and enthalpy change are $+200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}-\mathrm{mol}$ and $-2808 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.
solution:
For energy available to do useful biological work, we use Gibbs free energy change:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ $$

Given:

$$ \Delta H^\circ = -2808 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta S^\circ = +200 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

First convert entropy into kJ:

$$ 200 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} = 0.200 \ \text{kJ K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

Body temperature is:

$$ 37^\circ \text{C} = 37 + 273 = 310 \ \text{K} $$

Now substitute:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -2808 - (310)(0.200) $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -2808 - 62 $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -2870 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

The negative sign shows that energy is released. So, the energy available for muscular and nervous activity is:

$$ \boxed{2870 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}} $$

For $1.0$ mole of glucose, the available energy is:

$$ \boxed{2870 \ \text{kJ}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: For the combustion of sucrose at 298 K , the standard enthalpy change is $-5737 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, while the standard Gibbs energy change for the reaction is $-6333 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. On increasing the temperature to $29^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the extra work other than expansion work (in kJ ) which can be obtained is
question: For the combustion of sucrose at 298 K , the standard enthalpy change is $-5737 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, while the standard Gibbs energy change for the reaction is $-6333 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. On increasing the temperature to $29^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the extra work other than expansion work (in kJ ) which can be obtained is
solution:
For a reaction at constant temperature and pressure, the maximum non-expansion work obtainable is equal to the decrease in Gibbs energy:

$$ w_{\text{non-exp,max}}=-\Delta G $$

At $298\,\text{K}$,

$$ \Delta H^\circ=-5737\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ=-6333\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

We know,

$$ \Delta G^\circ=\Delta H^\circ-T\Delta S^\circ $$

So,

$$ \Delta S^\circ=\frac{\Delta H^\circ-\Delta G^\circ}{T} $$

Substituting the values,

$$ \Delta S^\circ=\frac{-5737-(-6333)}{298} $$

$$ \Delta S^\circ=\frac{596}{298}=2\,\text{kJ K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

Now the temperature is raised from $298\,\text{K}$ to $29^\circ\text{C}$.

$$ 29^\circ\text{C}=302\,\text{K} $$

Increase in temperature:

$$ \Delta T=302-298=4\,\text{K} $$

The change in Gibbs energy due to this rise in temperature is:

$$ \Delta(\Delta G)=-\Delta S^\circ \Delta T $$

$$ \Delta(\Delta G)=-(2)(4)=-8\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

So $\Delta G$ becomes $8\,\text{kJ}$ more negative. Hence, the additional non-expansion work obtainable is:

$$ 8\,\text{kJ} $$

So, the answer is:

$$ \boxed{8\,\text{kJ}} $$
